questions.question: What is the difference between propositional logic and first-order predicate logic? Give an example to illustrate.
keyPoints: Propositional logic deals with whole statements as atomic units
Predicate logic analyzes internal structure of statements
Predicate logic uses predicates, variables, and quantifiers
Example: 'All men are mortal' - propositional: P; predicate: ∀x(Man(x) → Mortal(x))

questions.question: Negate the following statements and express them in English: (a) ∀x (P(x) → Q(x)) (b) ∃x (R(x) ∧ S(x))
keyPoints: (a) ¬∀x ≡ ∃x ¬, so becomes ∃x (P(x) ∧ ¬Q(x)) - 'There exists some x such that P(x) is true and Q(x) is false'
(b) ¬∃x ≡ ∀x ¬, so becomes ∀x (¬R(x) ∨ ¬S(x)) - 'For all x, either R(x) is false or S(x) is false'
Apply De Morgan's laws for quantifiers

questions.question: What are free and bound variables? With an example, explain how a variable becomes bound.
keyPoints: Variable is bound if within scope of a quantifier (∀ or ∃)
Variable is free if not bound by any quantifier
Example: In ∀x (P(x) → ∃y Q(x,y)), x and y are bound
In P(x) ∧ ∃y R(y), x is free, y is bound

questions.question: Explain Universal Instantiation and Existential Generalization with examples.
keyPoints: UI: From ∀x P(x), infer P(c) for any constant c - 'All birds fly' → 'Sparrow flies'
EG: From P(c), infer ∃x P(x) - 'Socrates is mortal' → 'Someone is mortal'
UI allows moving from general to specific case
EG allows moving from specific to existential statement

questions.question: What is a well-formed formula (wff) in FOL? State the rules for constructing wffs.
keyPoints: wff is syntactically correct formula
Rules: Atomic formulas are wffs
If φ, ψ are wffs, then ¬φ, φ∧ψ, φ∨ψ, φ→ψ, φ↔ψ are wffs
If φ is wff and x is variable, then ∀x φ and ∃x φ are wffs
Parentheses used for grouping

questions.question: Determine whether the following argument is valid: All humans are mortal. Socrates is human. Therefore, Socrates is mortal.
keyPoints: Premise 1: ∀x (Human(x) → Mortal(x))
Premise 2: Human(Socrates)
By Universal Instantiation, Human(Socrates) → Mortal(Socrates)
By Modus Ponens: Mortal(Socrates)
Argument is valid - follows logical structure

questions.question: Translate the following English statements into FOL: (a) Every student passed some exam. (b) There is a person who loves everyone.
keyPoints: (a) ∀x (Student(x) → ∃y (Exam(y) ∧ Passed(x,y)))
(b) ∃x (Person(x) ∧ ∀y (Person(y) → Loves(x,y)))
Identify quantifiers and predicates carefully
Note: 'loves everyone' may mean everyone else or everyone including themselves

questions.question: Define and explain the four quantifier negation rules with examples. Show how ¬∀x P(x) is equivalent to ∃x ¬P(x) and ¬∃x P(x) is equivalent to ∀x ¬P(x).
keyPoints: Rule 1: ¬∀x φ ≡ ∃x ¬φ - 'Not all' means 'some not'
Rule 2: ¬∃x φ ≡ ∀x ¬φ - 'None' means 'all not'
Proof uses truth tables for quantifiers
Apply De Morgan's laws iteratively

questions.question: Explain the concept of valid arguments in predicate logic. Discuss Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Universal Instantiation, and Universal Generalization with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Valid argument: conclusion follows necessarily from premises
Modus Ponens: P→Q and P, therefore Q
Modus Tollens: P→Q and ¬Q, therefore ¬P
UI: From ∀x P(x) to P(c) - specific instance
UG: From P(c) to ∀x P(x), where c is arbitrary
Show how these combine in formal proofs

questions.question: What is meant by the 'domain of discourse'? Explain with examples how changing the domain affects the truth value of quantified statements. Also explain why specifying the domain is important.
keyPoints: Domain of discourse is the set of all objects under consideration
For '∃x (x < 0)' - true for integers, reals; false for natural numbers if 0 is included
For '∀x (x+0 = x)' - true for reals, false for some algebraic structures
Domain must be non-empty for standard FOL
Without domain, statements like ∃x P(x) are meaningless
Common domains: naturals, reals, persons, things

questions.question: Construct formal proofs for the following using inference rules: (a) From ∀x (P(x)→Q(x)) and ∀x P(x), derive ∀x Q(x). (b) From ∀x (R(x)→S(x)) and ∃x R(x), derive ∃x S(x).
keyPoints: (a) Proof: UI on ∀x P(x) gives P(a); UI on premise gives P(a)→Q(a); Modus Ponens gives Q(a); UG gives ∀x Q(x)
(b) Proof: EI on ∃x R(x) gives R(b); UI on premise gives R(b)→S(b); Modus Ponens gives S(b); EG gives ∃x S(x)
Show each step with justification
Emphasize when UI/EI and UG/EG can be applied
